titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Fred Jeremy Seligson Collection of Cid Corman and James Weil
Seligson (Fred Jeremy) Collection of Cid Corman and James Weil
titlestmt.author: by Beinecke staff

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Saturday November 21, 2020 at 04:02

did.unittitle: Fred Jeremy Seligson Collection of Cid Corman and James Weil

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Fred Jeremy Seligson on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund and the Ezra Pound Archive Fund, 2018-2019.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Fred Jeremy Seligson Collection of Cid Corman and James Weil is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Fred Jeremy Seligson Collection of Cid Corman and James Weil. Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

bioghist.head: Cid Corman (1924-2004) and James Weil (1929-2006)
bioghist.p: Cid Corman, born 29 June 1924 in Boston, Massachusetts to parents of Ukranian ancestry, was an American poet, translator, and editor. He was the author of more than 100 works, including Livingdying (1970), Belongings (1977), At their word (1978), In particular: poems new and selected (1986), and For crying out loud (2002), as well as multiple titles with Weil's Elizabeth Press. In 1958 he moved to Japan, where he lived much of the rest of his adult life. He died 12 March 2004 in Kyoto, Japan.

James L. Weil, born 15 June 1929 in New York to Morris, a financier, and Charlotte (Ullman) Weil, was an American poet and publisher. He attended the University of Chicago (1950) and Oxford University (1954) before going to work for the Dialight Corporation (1954-1968). Weil founded the Elizabeth Press (1963-1981), an important publisher of American poetry in the 1960s and 1970s, and published limited edition chapbooks and keepsakes under his own name from 1985 onward. His authors include William Bronk (1918-1999), Cid Corman (1924-2004), Larry Eigner (1927-1996), Theodore Enslin (1925-2011), Felix Stefanile (1920-2009), and others.
Summary information is also available in the standard print and online biographical resources.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection contains correspondence, writings by Cid Corman, and other papers. Series I and Series II includes correspondence to Seligson from Corman and James Weil and third party correspondence between Corman and Weil. There are 108 pieces of correspondence from Corman to Seligson dating from 1982 to 2002. Corman's letters discuss personal, literary, and cultural matters, as well as travel. Third party correspondence features approximately 332 pieces of correspondence from Weil to Corman from 1962 to 2002. Letters discuss a range of personal and professional matters and document Weil's work as a publisher, first with The Elizabeth Press (1963-1981), and then from the mid-1980s onward, as James L. Weil, Publisher. Series III. 2019 Addition includes additional correspondence between Corman and Weil, writings by Corman, and other papers. The series features drafts of more than 100 distinct works by Corman, including poems, essays, and other writings. Corman's writings in the series span from 1958 through 1999, though most drafts date from the 1960s and 1970s.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into three series: I. Letters to Fred Jeremy Seligson, 1982-2003. II. Third Party Correspondence, 1962-2002. III. 2019 Addition, 1958-2010.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I. Letters to Fred Jeremy Seligson features 108 pieces of correspondence from Corman to Seligson dating from 1982 to 2002. Corman's letters discuss personal, literary, and cultural matters, as well as travel. Corman and Seligson resided chiefly in Kyoto, Japan and Seoul, South Korea respectively during these years.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series II. Third party correspondence features approximately 332 pieces of correspondence from Weil to Corman over a period of four decades, from 1962 to 2002. Letters discuss a range of personal and professional matters and document Weil's work as a publisher, first with The Elizabeth Press (1963-1981), and then from the mid-1980s onward, as James L. Weil, Publisher. Enclosures, such as contracts, copies of writings, third party letters, clippings, and photographs, can be found among Weil's letters to Corman.

did.unittitle: Corman, Cid to James L. Weil
Weil, James L. to Kathryn Beam
Weil, James L. to Cid Corman
Weil, James L. to Cid Corman
Weil, James L. to Cid Corman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series III. 2019 Addition includes additional correspondence between Corman and Weil, writings by Corman, and other papers. The series features drafts of more than 100 distinct works by Corman, including poems, essays, and other writings. Writings include eleven notebooks, numbered III through XIII, of a multi-volume memoir of childhood. The volumes, autograph manuscript, appear to have been written over the course of a decade, from 1968 to 1978. Corman's writings in the series span from 1958 through 1999, though most drafts date from the 1960s and 1970s.

Many drafts of poems show revisions. Drafts, typescript, often show multiple dates, suggesting that the typescripts are transcriptions or revised drafts, with certain dates citing a previous, possibly original draft. Drafts with autograph manuscript corrections and/or annotations also appear to bear the dates of these revisions. For example, an untitled poem with one line of autograph manuscript corrections followed by the typed date sequence, "4 June 70 (4) from 8 Nov 66," and then the date "18 Apr 76," in Corman's hand, suggests that the draft typed out on 4 June 1970 was based on an earlier draft dated 8 November 1966, and that the autograph manuscript corrections were made 18 April 1976. Such date histories are accounted for the folder-level description below.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into three subseries: I. Correspondence, 1963-2010. II. Writings by Cid Corman, 1958-1999. III. Other Papers, 1965-2005.

did.unittitle: Corman, Cid to Lyndon B. Johnson
Corman, Cid to Fred Jeremy Seligson
Corman, Shizumi to Fred Jeremy Seligson
Weil, James L. to Cid Corman
Clippings removed from Weil, James L. to Cid Corman
["The aim is a poetry whose expression..."], essay, draft, typescript
"CC's: A new concept of business," essay, draft, typescript
"Dancing city," sketch for proposed film," draft, autograph manuscript
"The Dip," poem, draft, typescript, corrected and annotated
"Dooley the tout," poem, draft, corrected and annotated
"Family," treatment for proposed film, draft, typescript (photocopy)
"The First death of another," poem, draft, typescript, corrected
["I have been called arrogant..."], essay, draft, typescript, corrected
"The Last straw," poem, draft, autograph manuscript
"Man as the creature whose destiny is self-awareness," [essay?], draft, autograph manuscript
"The Nature of art," essay, draft, typescript, corrected
"The Odor of grapes," poem, draft, typescript
"On the translation of poetry," essay, draft, typescript
"On translation," essay, draft, typescript, corrected and annotated
"Poetry as necessity," essay, draft, typescript, corrected
["Poets of the world..."], essay, draft, typescript
["Proust in his famous research..."], essay, draft, typescript, corrected
"Relic costume drill," poem, draft, typescript, corrected
["There is need for an update on the Oedipus..."], note, draft, typescript
"The Thing itself," essay, draft, typescript, corrected and annotated
